docs.text: Lesson 07: Skill Files ¶ Previous lessons covered how to write effective prompts for individual tasks. But much of the context you need, your project's structure, the conventions you follow, the rules you want applied every time, is repetitive. Typing it again in every conversation is tedious and error-prone. A skill file solves this problem. It is a document that encodes persistent context for an LLM: the things the model should always know when working in a particular domain or on a particular project. You load it once at the start of a session and the model behaves consistently from that point. This lesson explains what skill files are, when to use them, how to read an existing one, and how to write your own. It also presents two ready-to-use examples: a general coding and documentation template, and a writing skill file built from the conventions in the Writing Papers track . What a skill file is ¶ A skill file is a text document, usually Markdown, that you pass to an LLM as persistent context. It is not a prompt for a single task. It is a briefing that applies to an entire session or workflow. The contents vary by purpose, but a useful skill file typically covers: Section What it contains Environment How to run the code; which interpreter, which commands Project structure Where things live; what each directory contains Conventions Style rules, naming conventions, forbidden patterns Workflow The sequence of steps for common tasks Checklists What to verify before a result is trusted In Claude Code and similar tools, skill files are stored in .claude/commands/ as Markdown files and are loaded by name when you invoke them with a slash command (e.g. /prometheus ). But skill files are not tool-specific: you can paste one into any LLM conversation as a system prompt or opening message. A real example: Prometheus ¶ The Prometheus neutrino simulation package uses a skill file at .claude/commands/prometheus.md . Here is the opening section: ## Environment **Python interpreter** (use paths relative to repo root — never rely on `python` or `python3` being on PATH): .prometheus_env/bin/python **Pytest binary:** .prometheus_env/bin/pytest **Activate for interactive terminal sessions:** source scripts/activate.sh .prometheus_env This one section prevents a common class of error: running the wrong Python. Without it, the LLM might call python and get a system Python with the wrong packages, producing confusing import errors. The skill file makes the correct behaviour automatic. Further down, the skill file documents the simulation pipeline: Prometheus.sim() └─ inject() ← LeptonInjector generates neutrino events └─ propagate() └─ OlympusPhotonPropagator.propagate(particle, rng_key) ├─ [tracks] generate_realistic_track() └─ [cascades] generate_cascade() And it records domain knowledge that is not in the source code: | Thing | Value / location | |---|---| | Distance cutoff | 300 m (normalizing flow not trained beyond this) | | Cascade resolution | 0.2 m longitudinal step | | Noise window | 5000 ns | These are the numbers a contributor needs to avoid subtle bugs, and they are exactly the kind of thing that is forgotten between sessions and expensive to re-derive. The skill file makes the model aware of them permanently. Finally, it records coding conventions: ## Docstring style NumPy format. Summary line must not start with "make". Use "create" or "build" for factory functions. This single rule prevents a whole class of inconsistency without requiring anyone to remember it. What the Prometheus skill file achieves ¶ A developer opening this project in a new session does not need to re-explain the environment, the architecture, or the naming conventions. The LLM consistently uses .prometheus_env/bin/python , writes NumPy docstrings, and respects the 300 m cutoff. The skill file is version-controlled alongside the code, so it stays accurate as the project evolves. A general coding and documentation template ¶ A reusable coding skill file template is provided at examples/skill-files/coding-docs-template.md . It covers the most important elements for any Python research project: environment setup, project layout, testing workflow, coding conventions, and documentation style. Adapt it for your project by filling in the bracketed placeholders. The key principle is: include anything that you find yourself explaining repeatedly, or anything that the model gets wrong if you do not say it explicitly. What to include in a coding skill file ¶ Environment ¶ ## Environment Python: `.venv/bin/python` (never use bare `python3`) Install: `.venv/bin/pip install -e .` Tests: `.venv/bin/pytest tests/ -x` Lint: `.venv/bin/ruff check src/` Project layout (list only the directories that matter to working tasks) ¶ ## Structure src/mypackage/ main package core.py [what it contains] analysis.py [what it contains] tests/ pytest test suite examples/ runnable scripts demonstrating key workflows Key invariants (the things the model should never violate) ¶ ## Invariants - All results in physical units (GeV, cm², etc.) — no dimensionless intermediates - Numpy random state always passed explicitly — no global seeds - Output arrays are always shape (N,) not (N,1) Docstring and style conventions ¶ ## Conventions - Docstrings: NumPy format - All public functions have type hints - No magic numbers in function bodies — define named constants at module level A writing skill file for physics papers ¶ For the context of writing and revising physics papers, skill files are equally powerful. The conventions of HEP writing, described in the Writing Papers track , are detailed enough that an LLM can apply them reliably if told exactly what they are. But they are also too numerous to type into every session. A writing skill file encodes these conventions once, so that every LLM interaction in a writing session automatically respects them. The file examples/skill-files/writing-hep.md is a full writing skill file based on: The conventions taught in the Writing Papers track (lessons 01–05) An exhaustive list of rules one learns to follow when writing with John Beacom What the writing skill file encodes ¶ The skill file is a condensed, LLM-optimised version of the style guide. It covers: Area Key rules encoded Voice First-person plural "we"; active verbs; never passive for key results Abstract 4–5 sentence structure; quantitative result required; no citations Introduction Four-paragraph structure; "In this work, we..." in paragraph 3; Figure 1 in paragraph 3 Paragraph structure Opening sentence states the main point; 3–6 sentences; no orphan sentences Results Figure-first ("Figure X shows..."); at least one OOM estimate per results section Conclusion Opens "We have shown/computed/demonstrated..."; no new results LaTeX Figure~\ref{} not Fig. ; Eq.~(\ref{}) not \eqref{} ; \mathrm{} for subscripts Figures 3-sentence caption structure; key takeaway in \textit{...} Citations Chronological order; max 3–4 per claim; no citations in abstract Forbidden phrases "As can be seen in Figure X..." → "Figure X shows..."; passive constructions How this connects to the Writing Papers track ¶ Track 03 teaches the rules through explanation and examples. The writing skill file operationalises those same rules as a persistent LLM context. The relationship is: Track 03 = understanding the rules Writing skill file = enforcing the rules in every session A student who has worked through track 03 can load the skill file and have the LLM check their draft against the exact conventions they have learned. A student who has not yet done track 03 will still get useful corrections from the skill file, and the corrections are a concrete entry point into the underlying rules. Using the writing skill file: two modes ¶ Mode 1: Drafting ¶ Load the skill file and give the model your notes or bullet points for a section. Ask it to draft the section following the style guide. [Skill file loaded] Draft the following as a Introduction paragraph 3 (the "In this work, we..." paragraph). My notes: - We study the sensitivity of Hyper-K to atmospheric neutrino flux - We compute event rates for CC and NC interactions - Main result: we reduce flux uncertainty from ~20% to ~7% - Figure 1 shows the ratio of measured to predicted flux as a function of energy The model will produce a paragraph that follows the style conventions without you having to specify them in the prompt. Mode 2: Review ¶ Load the skill file and paste a section of your draft. Ask the model to check it against the style rules and list violations. [Skill file loaded] Review the following Introduction section for style violations. For each violation, quote the offending text, name the rule, and suggest the fix. [paste your text] This is an interactive workflow, so you can ask follow-up questions about each flagged item. Example interaction ¶ Input: It is shown that the sensitivity of IceCube to the diffuse supernova neutrino background is significant. As can be seen in Figure 2, the expected event rate peaks at 20 MeV. The analysis is performed using the maximum likelihood method. Expected output from a model with the skill file loaded: Three violations: 1. VOICE: "It is shown that..." → "We show that..." (passive for key result) 2. FIGURE: "As can be seen in Figure 2..." → "Figure 2 shows..." (forbidden phrase) 3. VOICE: "The analysis is performed using..." → "We perform the analysis using..." (passive construction in body text) Without the skill file, the model might paraphrase these sentences without catching the violations. With the skill file, the specific rules are in its context and it applies them reliably. Writing your own skill file ¶ Start from what goes wrong ¶ The most valuable items in a skill file are the things the LLM gets wrong if you do not tell it. Start by listing: Commands or paths the model uses incorrectly. Style decisions the model makes wrong (units, variable names, docstring format). Domain invariants it violates (numerical cutoffs, sign conventions, required disclaimers). Workflow steps it skips. These are your highest-priority items. Keep it honest ¶ A skill file that is out of date is worse than no skill file: the model follows stale instructions and produces inconsistent output. Treat the skill file as part of the codebase or document: update it when the conventions change, and review it when a model starts behaving unexpectedly. Keep it concise ¶ A skill file that is too long will overwhelm the model's context and will not be read carefully. Aim for the minimum that prevents the most important errors. If a rule is already enforced by a linter or formatter, you do not need to repeat it in the skill file. Organise by role, not by tool ¶ Write the skill file for what the model needs to know, not for how the tool works. "Run tests with .venv/bin/pytest tests/ -x " is useful. "Claude Code supports skill files in .claude/commands/ " is not. That belongs in the documentation for the tool, not in the project skill file. Industrial context ¶ System prompts. In API-based deployments, the persistent context provided to an LLM is called a system prompt . It plays exactly the role of a skill file: it is loaded once and shapes every response in the session. Professional deployments version-control system prompts as carefully as application code, because a change to the system prompt can alter the behaviour of every downstream interaction. Prompt libraries. Large organisations maintain libraries of validated system prompts for different task types: customer support, code generation, document review. A skill file is the individual contributor's equivalent: a validated context document for a specific project or domain. RAG over style guides. Some organisations embed their style and convention documents in a retrieval system, so the model can look up the relevant rule at query time rather than having the entire document in context. For most research uses, a single well-maintained skill file is simpler and equally effective. Retrieval adds complexity without benefit at this scale. Evaluation and regression testing. When a skill file is updated, its effect on model behaviour can be measured by running a fixed set of test inputs and checking that the outputs respect the new conventions. For example one can run a set of test sections through the style checker and check that violations are correctly identified. For a hand-maintained skill file, periodic spot-checks serve the same purpose. Summary ¶ Concept Description Skill file A persistent context document loaded into an LLM session Why useful Avoids repeating context; enforces conventions; encodes domain knowledge Prometheus example Covers environment, architecture, pipeline, and coding conventions Coding template examples/skill-files/coding-docs-template.md Writing skill file examples/skill-files/writing-hep.md : encodes the conventions from the Writing Papers track Two modes Drafting mode (generate text that follows the style) and review mode (check violations) System prompt The industrial equivalent: persistent context in API deployments What to read next ¶ This is the final lesson in the track. Return to the track overview for the verification checklist from Lesson 06 , which applies to everything, including LLM-assisted revision of a draft checked against a skill file.
docs.title: Lesson 07 — Skill Files

docs.text: Previous lessons covered how to write effective prompts for individual tasks. But much of the context you need, your project's structure, the conventions you follow, the rules you want applied every time, is repetitive. Typing it again in every conversation is tedious and error-prone. A skill file solves this problem. It is a document that encodes persistent context for an LLM: the things the model should always know when working in a particular domain or on a particular project. You load it once at the start of a session and the model behaves consistently from that point. This lesson explains what skill files are, when to use them, how to read an existing one, and how to write your own. It also presents two ready-to-use examples: a general coding and documentation template, and a writing skill file built from the conventions in the Writing Papers track .
docs.title: Lesson 07: Skill Files

docs.text: A skill file is a text document, usually Markdown, that you pass to an LLM as persistent context. It is not a prompt for a single task. It is a briefing that applies to an entire session or workflow. The contents vary by purpose, but a useful skill file typically covers: Section What it contains Environment How to run the code; which interpreter, which commands Project structure Where things live; what each directory contains Conventions Style rules, naming conventions, forbidden patterns Workflow The sequence of steps for common tasks Checklists What to verify before a result is trusted In Claude Code and similar tools, skill files are stored in .claude/commands/ as Markdown files and are loaded by name when you invoke them with a slash command (e.g. /prometheus ). But skill files are not tool-specific: you can paste one into any LLM conversation as a system prompt or opening message.
docs.title: What a skill file is

docs.text: The Prometheus neutrino simulation package uses a skill file at .claude/commands/prometheus.md . Here is the opening section: ## Environment **Python interpreter** (use paths relative to repo root — never rely on `python` or `python3` being on PATH): .prometheus_env/bin/python **Pytest binary:** .prometheus_env/bin/pytest **Activate for interactive terminal sessions:** source scripts/activate.sh .prometheus_env This one section prevents a common class of error: running the wrong Python. Without it, the LLM might call python and get a system Python with the wrong packages, producing confusing import errors. The skill file makes the correct behaviour automatic. Further down, the skill file documents the simulation pipeline: Prometheus.sim() └─ inject() ← LeptonInjector generates neutrino events └─ propagate() └─ OlympusPhotonPropagator.propagate(particle, rng_key) ├─ [tracks] generate_realistic_track() └─ [cascades] generate_cascade() And it records domain knowledge that is not in the source code: | Thing | Value / location | |---|---| | Distance cutoff | 300 m (normalizing flow not trained beyond this) | | Cascade resolution | 0.2 m longitudinal step | | Noise window | 5000 ns | These are the numbers a contributor needs to avoid subtle bugs, and they are exactly the kind of thing that is forgotten between sessions and expensive to re-derive. The skill file makes the model aware of them permanently. Finally, it records coding conventions: ## Docstring style NumPy format. Summary line must not start with "make". Use "create" or "build" for factory functions. This single rule prevents a whole class of inconsistency without requiring anyone to remember it.
docs.title: A real example: Prometheus

docs.text: A developer opening this project in a new session does not need to re-explain the environment, the architecture, or the naming conventions. The LLM consistently uses .prometheus_env/bin/python , writes NumPy docstrings, and respects the 300 m cutoff. The skill file is version-controlled alongside the code, so it stays accurate as the project evolves.
docs.title: What the Prometheus skill file achieves

docs.text: A reusable coding skill file template is provided at examples/skill-files/coding-docs-template.md . It covers the most important elements for any Python research project: environment setup, project layout, testing workflow, coding conventions, and documentation style. Adapt it for your project by filling in the bracketed placeholders. The key principle is: include anything that you find yourself explaining repeatedly, or anything that the model gets wrong if you do not say it explicitly.
docs.title: A general coding and documentation template
What to include in a coding skill file

docs.text: ## Environment Python: `.venv/bin/python` (never use bare `python3`) Install: `.venv/bin/pip install -e .` Tests: `.venv/bin/pytest tests/ -x` Lint: `.venv/bin/ruff check src/`
docs.title: Environment

docs.text: ## Structure src/mypackage/ main package core.py [what it contains] analysis.py [what it contains] tests/ pytest test suite examples/ runnable scripts demonstrating key workflows
docs.title: Project layout (list only the directories that matter to working tasks)

docs.text: ## Invariants - All results in physical units (GeV, cm², etc.) — no dimensionless intermediates - Numpy random state always passed explicitly — no global seeds - Output arrays are always shape (N,) not (N,1)
docs.title: Key invariants (the things the model should never violate)

docs.text: ## Conventions - Docstrings: NumPy format - All public functions have type hints - No magic numbers in function bodies — define named constants at module level
docs.title: Docstring and style conventions

docs.text: For the context of writing and revising physics papers, skill files are equally powerful. The conventions of HEP writing, described in the Writing Papers track , are detailed enough that an LLM can apply them reliably if told exactly what they are. But they are also too numerous to type into every session. A writing skill file encodes these conventions once, so that every LLM interaction in a writing session automatically respects them. The file examples/skill-files/writing-hep.md is a full writing skill file based on: The conventions taught in the Writing Papers track (lessons 01–05) An exhaustive list of rules one learns to follow when writing with John Beacom
docs.title: A writing skill file for physics papers

docs.text: The skill file is a condensed, LLM-optimised version of the style guide. It covers: Area Key rules encoded Voice First-person plural "we"; active verbs; never passive for key results Abstract 4–5 sentence structure; quantitative result required; no citations Introduction Four-paragraph structure; "In this work, we..." in paragraph 3; Figure 1 in paragraph 3 Paragraph structure Opening sentence states the main point; 3–6 sentences; no orphan sentences Results Figure-first ("Figure X shows..."); at least one OOM estimate per results section Conclusion Opens "We have shown/computed/demonstrated..."; no new results LaTeX Figure~\ref{} not Fig. ; Eq.~(\ref{}) not \eqref{} ; \mathrm{} for subscripts Figures 3-sentence caption structure; key takeaway in \textit{...} Citations Chronological order; max 3–4 per claim; no citations in abstract Forbidden phrases "As can be seen in Figure X..." → "Figure X shows..."; passive constructions
docs.title: What the writing skill file encodes

docs.text: Track 03 teaches the rules through explanation and examples. The writing skill file operationalises those same rules as a persistent LLM context. The relationship is: Track 03 = understanding the rules Writing skill file = enforcing the rules in every session A student who has worked through track 03 can load the skill file and have the LLM check their draft against the exact conventions they have learned. A student who has not yet done track 03 will still get useful corrections from the skill file, and the corrections are a concrete entry point into the underlying rules.
docs.title: How this connects to the Writing Papers track
Using the writing skill file: two modes

docs.text: Load the skill file and give the model your notes or bullet points for a section. Ask it to draft the section following the style guide. [Skill file loaded] Draft the following as a Introduction paragraph 3 (the "In this work, we..." paragraph). My notes: - We study the sensitivity of Hyper-K to atmospheric neutrino flux - We compute event rates for CC and NC interactions - Main result: we reduce flux uncertainty from ~20% to ~7% - Figure 1 shows the ratio of measured to predicted flux as a function of energy The model will produce a paragraph that follows the style conventions without you having to specify them in the prompt.
docs.title: Mode 1: Drafting

docs.text: Load the skill file and paste a section of your draft. Ask the model to check it against the style rules and list violations. [Skill file loaded] Review the following Introduction section for style violations. For each violation, quote the offending text, name the rule, and suggest the fix. [paste your text] This is an interactive workflow, so you can ask follow-up questions about each flagged item.
docs.title: Mode 2: Review

docs.text: Input: It is shown that the sensitivity of IceCube to the diffuse supernova neutrino background is significant. As can be seen in Figure 2, the expected event rate peaks at 20 MeV. The analysis is performed using the maximum likelihood method. Expected output from a model with the skill file loaded: Three violations: 1. VOICE: "It is shown that..." → "We show that..." (passive for key result) 2. FIGURE: "As can be seen in Figure 2..." → "Figure 2 shows..." (forbidden phrase) 3. VOICE: "The analysis is performed using..." → "We perform the analysis using..." (passive construction in body text) Without the skill file, the model might paraphrase these sentences without catching the violations. With the skill file, the specific rules are in its context and it applies them reliably.
docs.title: Example interaction

docs.text: The most valuable items in a skill file are the things the LLM gets wrong if you do not tell it. Start by listing: Commands or paths the model uses incorrectly. Style decisions the model makes wrong (units, variable names, docstring format). Domain invariants it violates (numerical cutoffs, sign conventions, required disclaimers). Workflow steps it skips. These are your highest-priority items.

docs.text: A skill file that is out of date is worse than no skill file: the model follows stale instructions and produces inconsistent output. Treat the skill file as part of the codebase or document: update it when the conventions change, and review it when a model starts behaving unexpectedly.

docs.text: Concept Description Skill file A persistent context document loaded into an LLM session Why useful Avoids repeating context; enforces conventions; encodes domain knowledge Prometheus example Covers environment, architecture, pipeline, and coding conventions Coding template examples/skill-files/coding-docs-template.md Writing skill file examples/skill-files/writing-hep.md : encodes the conventions from the Writing Papers track Two modes Drafting mode (generate text that follows the style) and review mode (check violations) System prompt The industrial equivalent: persistent context in API deployments

docs.text: This is the final lesson in the track. Return to the track overview for the verification checklist from Lesson 06 , which applies to everything, including LLM-assisted revision of a draft checked against a skill file.
docs.title: What to read next

docs.text: Code of Conduct ¶ A code of conduct is the written agreement that defines how a community treats its members. It sets expectations for behaviour, explains what is not acceptable, and describes what happens when someone is harmed. This track explains why codes of conduct exist, what the Nestling community expects of its members, and how to act, or get help, when something goes wrong. Lessons ¶ Lesson Topic What you will learn 01: Understanding and Living the Code of Conduct Community standards Why codes of conduct matter, what the Nestling CoC requires, how to report an issue, and what consequences look like A healthy research environment does not happen by accident. It is built by people who take shared standards seriously and act on them when they need to.
docs.title: Overview

docs.text: A code of conduct is the written agreement that defines how a community treats its members. It sets expectations for behaviour, explains what is not acceptable, and describes what happens when someone is harmed. This track explains why codes of conduct exist, what the Nestling community expects of its members, and how to act, or get help, when something goes wrong.
docs.title: Code of Conduct

docs.text: Lesson Topic What you will learn 01: Understanding and Living the Code of Conduct Community standards Why codes of conduct matter, what the Nestling CoC requires, how to report an issue, and what consequences look like A healthy research environment does not happen by accident. It is built by people who take shared standards seriously and act on them when they need to.
docs.title: Lessons

docs.text: Lesson 01: Understanding and Living the Code of Conduct ¶ Research is a collaborative endeavour. You share offices, code, data, and ideas with people who have different backgrounds, expectations, and levels of power within the group. A code of conduct is the written agreement that makes those interactions workable for everyone, not just for those who already feel comfortable. This lesson explains what the Nestling Code of Conduct requires, why each part exists, and what to do if something goes wrong. Why codes of conduct exist ¶ Research environments have historically struggled with harassment, exclusion, and the abuse of power differentials. Research has consistently shown that hostile group climates drive talented people, disproportionately those from underrepresented groups, out of science. The harm is not only personal: it degrades the quality and breadth of research itself. A code of conduct does three things: Sets expectations before problems arise. It removes ambiguity about what is acceptable. "I didn't know that wasn't okay" is harder to claim when standards are written down and shared with everyone who joins. Gives people a path to act. Without a documented process, reporting feels risky and arbitrary. A code of conduct tells you exactly what to do and what will happen next. Signals who the community is for. Publishing a code of conduct is a statement that the community is actively committed to being safe and equitable, not just in principle, but in practice. Codes of conduct are not about policing speech The goal is not to create a list of forbidden words. It is to protect people's ability to participate fully in the community, to ask questions, share work, and disagree productively without being harassed or excluded. Our pledge ¶ The Nestling Code of Conduct opens with a pledge: We pledge to make our community welcoming, safe, and equitable for all. The word equitable is deliberate. Equal treatment means applying the same rules to everyone. Equitable treatment means recognising that people start from different positions and that identical treatment can produce unequal outcomes. A new PhD student and a senior researcher do not have the same power in an interaction. The pledge acknowledges that and asks everyone to factor it into how they behave. The pledge covers a broad list of characteristics: race, ethnicity, caste, age, neurodiversity, disability, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, language, religion, national origin, socio-economic background, and education level. This list is not exhaustive. The phrase "or other status" is intentional. If a characteristic shapes how someone is treated in the community, it falls within the scope of the pledge. Encouraged behaviours ¶ The CoC lists six encouraged behaviours: Behaviour Why it matters Engaging kindly and honestly Kindness without honesty produces empty validation. Honesty without kindness produces needless harm. Both together make feedback useful. Respecting different viewpoints Research improves when ideas compete on evidence, not on the seniority of who holds them. Taking responsibility for our actions Defensiveness after a mistake compounds the harm. Owning what you did is the first step toward repair. Giving and accepting constructive feedback Feedback is how research improves. Learning to give it well, and receive it without defensiveness, is a skill worth building deliberately. Committing to repairing harm when it occurs Harm happens even in well-functioning communities. What distinguishes healthy communities is the willingness to address it. Promoting the well-being of our community The community is a shared resource. Protecting it is everyone's responsibility, not just those in formal leadership roles. These are not aspirational extras. They describe the baseline standard of conduct expected from every member. Restricted behaviours ¶ The CoC identifies categories of behaviour that are never acceptable. Understanding what falls into each category matters, as incidents are sometimes ambiguous, and the categories help clarify whether something crosses the line. Harassment ¶ Harassment means violating explicitly expressed boundaries or continuing unwanted personal attention after a clear request to stop. The clearest example: someone says "please don't contact me outside of group meetings" and you continue to message them privately. But harassment does not require an explicit prior refusal. Repeated, unwanted attention, particularly attention that is personal rather than professional, is harassment even on a first occurrence if it is egregious enough. Character attacks ¶ Insulting, demeaning, or pejorative comments directed at a person. This includes sarcasm designed to humiliate, dismissing someone's work with contempt rather than substance, and public ridicule. The distinction from legitimate criticism: criticism addresses the work ("this methodology has a flaw because..."); a character attack addresses the person ("only someone incompetent would write code like this"). Stereotyping or discrimination ¶ Characterising someone's personality or behaviour on the basis of their identity or traits, race, gender, nationality, disability, and so on. In research settings this often appears as low expectations ("surprising that you got this far"), dismissiveness ("this isn't really a field for people like you"), or assumptions about competence based on background rather than demonstrated ability. Sexualisation ¶ Behaviour that would generally be considered inappropriately intimate given the professional context of the community. This includes comments about physical appearance, sharing explicit content, or unsolicited expressions of romantic interest in a professional setting. Violating confidentiality ¶ Sharing or acting on personal or private information without permission. In a research group this includes disclosing a colleague's health situation, immigration status, family circumstances, or salary without their consent. Endangerment ¶ Causing, encouraging, or threatening physical harm or violence toward any person or group. Other restrictions ¶ The CoC also restricts: Misleading identity : impersonating someone else, or pretending to be someone else to evade enforcement. Failing to credit sources : not properly attributing the ideas, code, or writing of others. In research, this overlaps directly with academic integrity. Promotional materials : unsolicited commercial content shared in community spaces. Irresponsible communication : sharing content that describes, links to, or promotes any of the restricted behaviours above. Instances, threats, and promotion are all violations You do not have to personally commit an act to violate the CoC. Threatening a restricted behaviour, or encouraging or promoting it, is itself a violation. Scope ¶ The CoC applies within all community spaces: the repository, issue tracker, pull requests, group meetings, and any communication channel used by the group. It also applies when a member is officially representing the community externally, at a conference, in a public forum, or as a named contributor on a publication. Behaviour in personal spaces is generally outside the scope, unless it creates a situation that affects someone's ability to participate safely in the community. CoCs apply far beyond software repositories Codes of conduct are standard practice in many settings: academic conferences, workshops, summer schools, reading groups, online forums, and research collaborations. If you organise or attend any of these, you are likely already operating under one, or should be. The principles in this lesson transfer directly to all of those contexts. Reporting an issue ¶ Reporting is the mechanism by which the CoC functions in practice. A code of conduct that no one reports violations of provides no protection. Who to contact ¶ Report possible violations by email to the maintainer: stephan.meighenberger@gmail.com . What to include ¶ You do not need to provide a perfect account. Include as much as you know: What happened, as specifically as you can describe it. When and where it happened (a GitHub link, a meeting date, a Slack channel). Who was involved (the person whose conduct is in question, any witnesses). Any messages, screenshots, or other evidence you have access to. If you are not sure whether something is a violation, report it anyway. It is better to raise something that turns out not to be a violation than to leave a genuine one unreported. What happens after you report ¶ Your report is received and acknowledged promptly. The situation is investigated, which may involve speaking to others involved. An enforcement decision is made, guided by the ladder below. You are informed of the outcome to the extent that confidentiality allows. Investigation and enforcement are kept as transparent as possible while prioritising the safety and confidentiality of everyone involved, especially the person who reported. You will not be penalised for reporting in good faith Retaliation against someone who reports a possible violation is itself a violation of the CoC. If you experience retaliation after reporting, report that too. Consequences and the enforcement ladder ¶ The CoC uses an enforcement ladder: a graduated sequence of consequences that scales with the severity and pattern of violations. Depending on the severity of a single incident, lower rungs may be skipped entirely. Warning ¶ When used : A single incident or limited series of incidents. What it looks like : A private, written message that explains what behaviour violated the CoC and what is expected going forward. Repair : A private written acknowledgement of responsibility, and a clear commitment to changed behaviour. Warnings exist because many violations are not malicious. Someone may not have realised their behaviour was causing harm. A warning gives them the opportunity to understand and change. It is not a punishment. It is a signal that something needs to change. Temporary suspension ¶ When used : A pattern of repeated violations that warnings have not resolved. What it looks like : A private written notification that access to community spaces is suspended for a defined period, with stated conditions for return. Repair : Respecting the spirit of the suspension and meeting the conditions for return. Re-entry into the community carries an expectation of genuine change, not just serving a waiting period. Permanent ban ¶ When used : A pattern of violations that other steps have failed to resolve, or a single violation so serious that no other response can keep the community safe. What it looks like : Access to all community spaces, tools, and communication channels is removed. Repair : There is no repair path for violations at this level. The ladder is a guideline, not a rigid script Enforcement is ultimately a matter of judgment. The ladder does not prevent skipping rungs for a sufficiently serious violation, nor does it require escalation if earlier steps have been effective. The goal at every step is to repair harm and protect the community, not to punish mechanically. What you can do ¶ The CoC is not only for extraordinary situations. It shapes everyday interactions. If you observe a violation : You are not required to intervene in the moment if that feels unsafe. You can document what you witnessed and report it, or offer support to the person affected and encourage them to report. If you are the person harmed : You do not have to decide alone whether what happened was "bad enough" to report. Report it. Let the process make that determination. If you are told your behaviour violated the CoC : Resist the impulse to dispute the framing immediately. Listen first. A warning is an opportunity to understand how your actions affected someone, which is more valuable than a clean record. If you are unsure : Ask. An informal conversation about whether something is appropriate is far better than an unaddressed incident that damages trust over time. What to read next ¶ The full Code of Conduct is the authoritative document. This lesson explains it, but the CoC itself is what governs. For broader context on building inclusive research environments, the Mozilla Community Participation Guidelines and the Contributor Covenant are widely used references that informed the Nestling CoC.
docs.title: Lesson 01 — Understanding and Living the Code of Conduct

docs.text: Causing, encouraging, or threatening physical harm or violence toward any person or group.
docs.title: Endangerment

docs.text: The CoC also restricts: Misleading identity : impersonating someone else, or pretending to be someone else to evade enforcement. Failing to credit sources : not properly attributing the ideas, code, or writing of others. In research, this overlaps directly with academic integrity. Promotional materials : unsolicited commercial content shared in community spaces. Irresponsible communication : sharing content that describes, links to, or promotes any of the restricted behaviours above. Instances, threats, and promotion are all violations You do not have to personally commit an act to violate the CoC. Threatening a restricted behaviour, or encouraging or promoting it, is itself a violation.
docs.title: Other restrictions

docs.text: The CoC applies within all community spaces: the repository, issue tracker, pull requests, group meetings, and any communication channel used by the group. It also applies when a member is officially representing the community externally, at a conference, in a public forum, or as a named contributor on a publication. Behaviour in personal spaces is generally outside the scope, unless it creates a situation that affects someone's ability to participate safely in the community. CoCs apply far beyond software repositories Codes of conduct are standard practice in many settings: academic conferences, workshops, summer schools, reading groups, online forums, and research collaborations. If you organise or attend any of these, you are likely already operating under one, or should be. The principles in this lesson transfer directly to all of those contexts.
docs.title: Scope

docs.text: Reporting is the mechanism by which the CoC functions in practice. A code of conduct that no one reports violations of provides no protection.
docs.title: Reporting an issue

docs.text: Report possible violations by email to the maintainer: stephan.meighenberger@gmail.com .
docs.title: Who to contact

docs.text: You do not need to provide a perfect account. Include as much as you know: What happened, as specifically as you can describe it. When and where it happened (a GitHub link, a meeting date, a Slack channel). Who was involved (the person whose conduct is in question, any witnesses). Any messages, screenshots, or other evidence you have access to. If you are not sure whether something is a violation, report it anyway. It is better to raise something that turns out not to be a violation than to leave a genuine one unreported.
docs.title: What to include

docs.text: Your report is received and acknowledged promptly. The situation is investigated, which may involve speaking to others involved. An enforcement decision is made, guided by the ladder below. You are informed of the outcome to the extent that confidentiality allows. Investigation and enforcement are kept as transparent as possible while prioritising the safety and confidentiality of everyone involved, especially the person who reported. You will not be penalised for reporting in good faith Retaliation against someone who reports a possible violation is itself a violation of the CoC. If you experience retaliation after reporting, report that too.
docs.title: What happens after you report

docs.text: The CoC uses an enforcement ladder: a graduated sequence of consequences that scales with the severity and pattern of violations. Depending on the severity of a single incident, lower rungs may be skipped entirely.
docs.title: Consequences and the enforcement ladder

docs.text: When used : A single incident or limited series of incidents. What it looks like : A private, written message that explains what behaviour violated the CoC and what is expected going forward. Repair : A private written acknowledgement of responsibility, and a clear commitment to changed behaviour. Warnings exist because many violations are not malicious. Someone may not have realised their behaviour was causing harm. A warning gives them the opportunity to understand and change. It is not a punishment. It is a signal that something needs to change.
docs.title: Warning

docs.text: When used : A pattern of repeated violations that warnings have not resolved. What it looks like : A private written notification that access to community spaces is suspended for a defined period, with stated conditions for return. Repair : Respecting the spirit of the suspension and meeting the conditions for return. Re-entry into the community carries an expectation of genuine change, not just serving a waiting period.
docs.title: Temporary suspension

docs.text: When used : A pattern of violations that other steps have failed to resolve, or a single violation so serious that no other response can keep the community safe. What it looks like : Access to all community spaces, tools, and communication channels is removed. Repair : There is no repair path for violations at this level. The ladder is a guideline, not a rigid script Enforcement is ultimately a matter of judgment. The ladder does not prevent skipping rungs for a sufficiently serious violation, nor does it require escalation if earlier steps have been effective. The goal at every step is to repair harm and protect the community, not to punish mechanically.
docs.title: Permanent ban

docs.text: The CoC is not only for extraordinary situations. It shapes everyday interactions. If you observe a violation : You are not required to intervene in the moment if that feels unsafe. You can document what you witnessed and report it, or offer support to the person affected and encourage them to report. If you are the person harmed : You do not have to decide alone whether what happened was "bad enough" to report. Report it. Let the process make that determination. If you are told your behaviour violated the CoC : Resist the impulse to dispute the framing immediately. Listen first. A warning is an opportunity to understand how your actions affected someone, which is more valuable than a clean record. If you are unsure : Ask. An informal conversation about whether something is appropriate is far better than an unaddressed incident that damages trust over time.
docs.title: What you can do

docs.text: The full Code of Conduct is the authoritative document. This lesson explains it, but the CoC itself is what governs. For broader context on building inclusive research environments, the Mozilla Community Participation Guidelines and the Contributor Covenant are widely used references that informed the Nestling CoC.
docs.title: What to read next

docs.text: Working Together: Your Role ¶ The research group environment is unlike most other working environments you will have encountered. Hours are flexible, no one is monitoring your attendance, and the relationship with your supervisor is often far more informal than in industry. This is one of the things that makes research genuinely enjoyable. It also comes with a responsibility that is easy to underestimate at the start. Participation and responsiveness ¶ Flexibility of schedule is not the same as freedom from participation. Group meetings, seminars, journal clubs, and informal conversations are not optional extras. They are where a significant part of your scientific education happens. The insights, habits, and ways of thinking you absorb by being present in a research group are not available any other way. Being reachable matters too. Respond to emails and messages within a reasonable timeframe. If your supervisor or collaborators cannot get hold of you, it creates friction and erodes trust. Physical presence norms vary by group, but showing up to group events and being reachable are universal expectations. Communicate before you disappear ¶ When you are stuck, and you will be repeatedly, say so early. Do not wait until a problem has been sitting for weeks before mentioning it. A supervisor can help you with a blockage in week two; by week eight it is a crisis. This applies to progress more broadly. Come to meetings with an honest account of where things stand, including what has not worked. "I have not made progress and I am not sure why" is far more useful than a vague update that papers over a real problem. Prepare for meetings ¶ Your meeting time is your time. Use it. Come with a short agenda: what you have done, what is blocking you, what you need. A prepared student gets significantly more out of supervision than one who arrives hoping the supervisor will drive the conversation. Engage broadly ¶ At the start of a PhD it is easy to feel overwhelmed by your own project and to retreat into it, skipping seminars that are not directly related to your work, avoiding journal clubs, staying away from activities that feel tangential. This is a trap. Breadth is not a distraction from your research. It is part of it. Seminars on adjacent topics train you to follow an argument in unfamiliar territory, exactly the skill your own work demands. The people in the room are future collaborators and colleagues. The conversations over coffee after a talk are often where the most useful things happen. Engaging with the field beyond your narrow project is how you become a researcher, not just someone who did one specific analysis. Take feedback constructively ¶ Feedback on your writing, your analysis, and your presentations is how you improve. It is not always easy to receive, especially when you have invested significant effort. The goal is to hear it without defensiveness: understand what is being said, ask questions if something is unclear, and use it. How feedback is delivered is on your supervisor. How you receive it is on you. Everything takes longer than you expect ¶ This is not a comment on your work ethic. It is a structural fact about research that catches almost every new student off guard. In coursework, there is always an answer key. You solve the problem, check it against the expected result, and move on. In research, there is no external check. The validity of your result is your responsibility, and establishing that validity requires cross-checks: arriving at the same answer through independent methods, verifying your code against known limits, testing edge cases, reproducing published results before extending them. Cross-checks are not optional polish. They are the standard of proof. Your supervisor will not audit every line of your code or every step of your analysis; that ownership is yours. This means that getting an answer is the beginning of the work, not the end. Budget accordingly. When something is taking longer than you expected, the first question to ask is whether you have actually finished validating it. The answer is usually no.
docs.title: Working Together: Your Role

docs.text: Working Together: Your Supervisor ¶ Supervision relationships in research take many forms. The style, frequency of contact, and level of involvement vary enormously between groups, institutions, and individuals. There is no single template that works for everyone. That said, some basics tend to hold across the field. This page names them, not as rules, but as a frame for what to look for and what to expect. What supervision typically involves ¶ A research supervisor is responsible for scientific direction, feedback, and professional development. In practice this usually means: Regular meetings with some defined minimum frequency Feedback on your written work and presentations Guidance on which problems to pursue and how to approach them Introductions to the broader community: collaborators, conferences, other groups Honest advice about your progress and your career options These things vary in form. Some supervisors are very hands-on; others give significant independence from the start. Neither style is inherently wrong. What matters is that you know which one you have and can work with it. Group structure ¶ In many groups, especially large ones, your day-to-day supervision may come from a postdoc or a senior PhD student rather than the professor directly. This is normal and often beneficial. They are closer to the work and more accessible. The same expectations apply to this relationship, and the same issues can arise. If a problem emerges at this level and cannot be resolved, the appropriate step is to bring it to the professor or group leader. Common failure modes ¶ Supervisors are scientists first. Most were never trained to supervise, and the problems students experience are common, well-documented, and structural, not personal failures or signs that you have a uniquely bad situation. You are not alone. The most frequently reported problems in the field are: Availability. A supervisor who is rarely reachable, cancels meetings repeatedly, or goes weeks without meaningful contact leaves students without direction. This is the single most common complaint. Unclear expectations. Students often do not know what their thesis needs to contain, what "done" looks like, or how close they are to finishing. If this is never stated explicitly, ask directly. It is a reasonable question and you are entitled to a real answer. Slow feedback on written work. Drafts can sit for weeks. This is common enough that it is worth agreeing on a turnaround expectation early, before you submit a draft, not after. Career guidance. Supervisors teach the science but often do not give honest guidance on careers: job market realities, networking, how to position yourself. This gap is common. If it is not coming from your supervisor, seek other sources: senior postdocs, other faculty, professional societies. Credit and visibility. Your contributions should be visible. This includes authorship on papers, being sent to give talks, and being introduced to collaborators. Consistent invisibility is worth raising. Service work. In large experimental collaborations, service work, such as detector shifts, software maintenance, and computing tasks, can consume a student's time without contributing to their thesis. A good supervisor protects their students from being over-assigned to this. If it is happening to you, it is a legitimate thing to raise. The two extremes. Some supervisors micromanage; others disengage after the first year once a student can work independently. Both are problems. The goal is a relationship that gives increasing autonomy over time while remaining genuinely engaged. Feedback culture. Harsh, dismissive, or belittling feedback is not a sign of high standards. It is a failure of supervision. Critical feedback delivered with respect is normal and expected; feedback that makes you feel worthless is not. Power imbalance. Your supervisor writes your recommendation letters. This dynamic is real, and it is one of the main reasons students stay silent about problems. Knowing that this pressure exists is the first step to not being controlled by it. Institution policy is the overriding authority ¶ Your university or research institution has formal policies on supervision, grievances, and student welfare. These take precedence over any informal arrangement or group culture, including anything written here. Know where to find them. The key signal ¶ If something is wrong, the right first step is almost always to raise it with your supervisor directly. Most problems can be resolved this way, and most supervisors respond well to a student who comes to them honestly. If raising it with your supervisor does not feel possible, that is when there is an actual problem. At that point, the right move is to escalate: the graduate coordinator, a second supervisor, the department ombudsperson, or another trusted faculty member. You should not have to manage a broken supervision relationship alone. The structures to help you exist. Find out what they are at your institution before you need them.
docs.title: Working Together: Your Supervisor
